Second, who here is ever unsubscribed from their friend's number who text them? And the reason for that is because we see connect, well, nope. And if you think about why 101 customers stay the longest, okay, so anyone here ever trained 101? Right. How many customers you lose? They paid the most. And then for people who have only done semi-private or who have done HCP, what's your turn on those? And it's because the level of communication, the amount of communication cycles that we have with 101 customers and 104 customers, email their list because they don't want people to cancel. Oh, I know you would. That's lying. And what you have to do is you have to shake the tree and let the few apples that are going to fall fall. And then you have to restart that relationship and restart the kind of communication. So, all right, I'm going to get into this. So retention number two, I'm going to get through all six and then we'll go deep. Gennifer two, systemize, don't automate. So whenever you see that there's an automated message on your phone or something like that, usually you stop paying attention, right? Stop paying attention to it. And so I can't we have longer texts to skip you up because then it doesn't seem organic and it doesn't seem like a real one-to-one personal communication. Like I don't send, if someone sends me a paragraph, I'm already upset with them. Communication cycles with as many customers as you can. So it's like you don't want to manually text everybody, right? So that's why we have tools like skip you, etc. But we don't want to automate the whole thing, right? And so that's why having a balance between outbound automation but human inbound response is like this beautiful marriage of the two. So people feel like you're checking in on them and when they respond they actually get a real person. And so it kind of balances you which is why you don't want to have everything that sounds pre-canned and I'll get to that. Prisoner 3. Create and this is just kind of a summary of one-to-one experience with us from a communication standpoint. We can still retain them, right? And what Ben and Dean will talk about and I'm going to talk about too is that it's always in the details, right? It's always like the devil impacts people and the good thing is that the only thing that's harder to do than the little things, sorry, the only thing easier to do than the little things is nothing. So you just need one little level of effort and then you can start taking care of this stuff. Prisoner 5. They don't care how much you know until you know how much you... That. And that goes two-fold. And one of the things that I don't know if Layla hit on it because my brain's wired. But she represents the exact same thing as I get to our employees. She sends them handwritten cards, she sends them gifts, she tells them how amazing they are. We write, like, surprise, like, so we're like, Mel, you saved the day. You rocked that, right? And so giving people that kind of recognition is super, super important. Sorry, I'm going off track on this one. But just like in the beginning of this, I give you the origin story. Does anyone here feel more connected with my story now? You didn't know that. Okay. Your employees and your customers will feel the same way. And this is like a Dean Kennedy principle that I've really taken home and that there's multiple levels of stories, right? There's parables that you can use to explain itself, which is really cool. You can break beliefs with that. You can use stories to position within a marketplace, within a discussion, which is what the setter does, right? We're telling the story of this person, right? And they believe the story and then they buy. The third type is really an origin story. Like, where did you come from? What is the path? What is the journey that you've walked to get here? And when you tell that to your customers and you tell it over and over and over again, you start to get better and better and better at telling the story. Ladies, I can tell you our story in my sleep because she says it to every new employee that comes on board so that they are all bought into the same story, right? We have the same origin. We have the same goal. And so it's like, from cradle to grave, when she walks them through that, it's them having the epiphany bridge and going through those struggles, wing it because they get the path that got us there. Does that make sense? Okay. So, retention. This is the main presentation. I just wanted to say those things. I had them this morning and I was like, what am I going to say? Retention. Measure of how good your business actually is and how much impact you actually make. So some basic math. 7% attrition rate. And you sign up 10 people a month. You max out at 143 EFTs. It's 10 divided by .07. That's the math. It's easy to grow. You're like, I had zero customers. No, I have 10, right? And month two, I have 19. I lost one, 7%, right? And month three, I add 10 and I lose still probably one. So I'm not at 28, right? You see how the math kind of starts adding up and then that little bit starts budding out more and more. And then the moment that 10 equals .07, we're going to get too messy, then that means that it's in balance, which means you don't grow anymore. I want to illustrate this because the difference between a 7% attrition rate and a 3% attrition rate. Big deal, right? Big, big N has a sub 5% attrition rate. Each one of those percentage points between 7 and 3, that means that he's going to have two and a half times the size of the business, right? It's really big. It's really important. So when you're like, man, these people are leaving, like, he has sub 5%. People aren't getting injured. They're not moving. Their jobs didn't change. They're lying. It's in the 90s. People move, right? So retention. Here's point number one. The golden ratio. We have the badges that have the gold thingy-jean on them. The number one thing that you can aspire to in business is if you can get your referral percentage higher than your turn percentage, means it's higher than equal to one, which means more people will refer people to your business than people who will leave your business. When you achieve that, you don't need ads. You don't need anything. You just grow like a virus, right? Or something not bad. That's the secret to organic growth. Right now, and this is a little story I'm just going to tell you, not to inflate ourselves, but we accidentally, because there was a while where I just wasn't checking your ads for weeks on end, Lately came to me and she was like, the application seemed a little bit lower this week. I was like, well, how many did we get? She was like, 65. I was like, okay. I'll take a look at the ads. So I pulled it up on my phone at that moment. Our ads have been shut down for 12 days and it's this weird sense of comfort where you're like, we don't need to market it. We can because we want to add gasoline to it, but we don't need to. We can still grow. And that's my honest goal for you because when you have that metric, when you have referral percentage overturned higher than one, you won. You won a business for this moment. You won because it means that you can grow and there's literally nothing stopping. You've made your service so good that more people will say, I will get more people in the door than people want to leave. So that's the viral way of growing the business. Now I would give you my Chipotle, ClickFunnels, and UberStory, but the reason that Uber had such a viral growth is that they had two levels of client outcomes, which is why Alex is so excited about our business because you want to have as many levels of client outcomes as you can. Alex went, how you can have multiple levels of outcomes within the weight loss business? You change a mother's life and then you change her kids' lives. You have multiple levels of impact. That's value. And so we're very grateful to be able to give you Dr. Cashi's mind for nutrition and all the best training stuff in the world so that no other means of income to have mine, which is awesome. And so if you can look at your business from an impact standpoint and see how can we drive the roots deeper and deeper into our clients' lives, you'll become more valuable to them, and then the more lives that you can impact, it will always reflect on your income. So divide your clients into four blocks. ABCD. Let's say you've got 100 people. Alright. ABCD. That's all of your customers that you have. Step two. You have to turn each block into a printout with client info. Okay? So that means their name, phone number, address. Alright? And you have got the checkboxes. Four weeks, four checkboxes, and then you've got one weekly for your handwritten card. Assign each to a trainer. Most people have four trainers. Ish. Right? Plus add... You've got four people who you can divvy this up to. And if you want to, remember servant leader, you can take it, just to leave by example. Okay? Now, client block A gets trainer one, client block B gets trainer two, C3, D4, and then what happens next month? You rotate. So that the same customer doesn't get the same handwritten card and the same trainer every month. They get a little too close and they start walking with your trainer. Right? So you rotate trainers so that that doesn't happen. Step four. Have the trainers divide the client blocks into four chunks. There it is. And so the way that we do this is that now, maybe before, so we had 100 total clients. We've got four trainers. Each trainer is 25 people. And so for week one, they're going to send six handwritten cards. It's about a minute and a half. It's not taking. But now, for 18 minutes a week, every single customer I have in my gym gets a handwritten card. Do you think that for the cost, it breaks down to this? Because someone's going to be curious. It breaks down to about a buck of card if you're doing it on your own. And it also, who thinks that their trainers will appreciate their customers? So we're teaching them, literally writing how much they appreciate them. They start acting that way. So here's the labor cost.
